title: Needle-felted pony
subtitle: A compact Exmoor pony in brown merino with a wire-armature body for poseable legs
excerpt: Build a stocky Exmoor-type pony in warm brown and cream merino using a wire armature so the legs can be posed. The finished pony stands about 10 cm tall and has a short mane and tail made from roving tufts.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional needle-felting practice. Wire-armature quadruped forms are a standard intermediate British needle-felting project; the body-building sequence follows standard guild workshop technique.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry felting method using barbed needles to tangle wool fibres together without heat or water. Each stab locks fibres into a progressively firmer mass.
A wire frame inside a needle-felted piece that allows the piece to be posed. For a four-legged animal, a single looped wire forms both front legs; a second forms both hind legs.
Wool fibre drawn into a continuous ribbon. The standard input material for needle felting.

content.text: because four standing legs cannot support a body without internal structure. The wire goes in first and
is built up around it in layers. This takes longer than a simple rounded animal but produces a piece that stands and can be posed.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. Keep a finger guard on the supporting hand at all times.

items.name: Cream or pale gold merino roving (muzzle, belly stripe)
Dark brown merino roving (mane, tail, hooves)
Craft wire or pipe-cleaner wire (1.5 mm)

content.text: Cut one 30 cm length of wire for the front legs and one for the hind legs. Fold each in half so the central bend becomes the top of the leg pair (at the shoulder and hip respectively). Twist the two wires together in the centre to form the spine, with the front-leg loop pointing forward and the hind-leg loop pointing backward. You should now have four legs projecting downward and a short spine between the two wire centres.

Wrap each wire leg in a thin layer of brown roving, working from the top of the leg downward. Secure the roving by needling along the wrapped length. This prevents the legs from spinning in the wire loops later. Wrap the spine section similarly.

Needle felt a barrel-shaped body core around the wire spine, building up layers of brown roving until the barrel is about 8 cm long and 5 cm in diameter. The neck rises from one end and the haunches from the other. Add more roving at the haunches to give the rounded hindquarter shape that is characteristic of a pony.
Step 4: Build the head and neck

Needle felt a separate head: a blunt-nosed rectangle about 4 cm long tapering to the muzzle. Needle the head to a reasonably firm density, then attach it at the top of the neck by needling through the base of the head into the neck repeatedly from several angles. Add a cream muzzle patch and needle it in.

Pull short tufts of dark brown roving for the mane. Lay them in a line along the top of the neck and needle each one at the root only, leaving the tips free to form the mane. Attach a larger tuft at the rump for the tail the same way. Needle small hooves in dark brown at the base of each wire leg.

items.cause: The legs are not even in length or the wire was not spread at the base for stability
items.fix: Check all four legs are the same height. Bend the wire at the base of each leg outward by about 5 mm to form a slight foot that grips the surface better.

items.problem: The wire pokes through at the knees or hips
items.cause: The roving layer over the armature is too thin at the wire-fold points
items.fix: Add extra roving over any thin area and needle it in gradually. Wire bends are the hardest points to cover; work them slowly with a coarser needle first, then refine with a finer one.
